summary: The prerequisite chain itself is sound: the modular-arithmetic stub is honest and its registry link resolves to a real 3-frame figure; asymmetric cryptography and prime numbers are each taught self-contained before the target, and the order (prerequisites first, target last) is correct. But the arc breaks at the finish line. The RSA section's only frame is a dependency-convergence diagram (three labeled boxes with arrows into an 'RSA encryption' box) — it restates that the three ideas combine without ever showing how. A reader finishes knowing nothing about RSA beyond what the asymmetric-cryptography section already taught; RSA's own definition in the page is indistinguishable from the generic two-key definition. The graph file itself declared RSA non-atomic because 'no single short figure can carry all of that', yet the chain ends with exactly one short figure that carries none of it.
gaps: Broken arc: the target section never teaches RSA's mechanism. There is no frame showing key generation (multiply two secret primes to get the public modulus), no frame showing encryption/decryption as wrap-around exponentiation, and no frame showing why the scheme is hard to reverse. The single RSA frame is a meta-diagram of the prerequisites, so the chain builds three foundations and then never assembles them.

Unmet prerequisite: the difficulty of factoring is the security core of RSA and is explicitly invoked in the builds-on text ('its safety rests on how hard it is to find those primes again'), but it is never illustrated, linked, or stubbed anywhere in the chain. The prime-numbers figure shows what a prime is, not why un-multiplying two large primes is hard.

Leap: the chain jumps from clock-style wrap-around counting (the modular-arithmetic figure stops at '14 o'clock is 2 o'clock') to the claim that wrap-around math can lock and unlock messages ('the lock-and-unlock math', 'wrap-around math turns it'). Nothing bridges counting on a dial to a computation that scrambles a message one way and unscrambles it only with a second key.

Redundant target definition: the RSA definition on the page ('a public key anyone can use to lock... a private key... to unlock') is functionally identical to the asymmetric-cryptography definition, so the page never articulates what distinguishes RSA from the general concept it supposedly instantiates.
